blocks.content: The afternoon next door had gone quiet. Julian could feel it more than hear it — the absence of the sharp overlapping voices from the morning. People had separated. The house had stopped trying to contain the conversation and let everyone drift to their own corners.
He went for a walk at three. The beach was mostly empty — midweek, shoulder season, the vacationers mostly gone. He walked south, the same direction he'd walked Friday night.
He stopped where the bonfire had been.

Just a ring of sand now. Ash and the ghost of warmth that wasn't there anymore. He stood at the edge of it and looked back at the beach house from the angle he'd had Friday night — the distance, the firelight, the six shapes.
He oriented himself. Where the fire had been. Where the six people had been arranged around it. Where he'd been walking.
From this angle. Moving south. The fire to my left. Two people close to the flames, sitting forward. One standing just behind. One with her back to the water. One at the far right edge.
And one — slightly behind the others, turned not toward the fire but toward the person at the edge. Consistently. The whole time I was in sightline.
He looked at the ash ring.
He pulled out his notepad and sketched the arrangement from memory. Six positions. The fire. The angles.
